title: Gut microbiota in acute lung injury/acute respiratory distress syndrome: mechanistic insights and therapeutic opportunities via the gut-lung axis
journal: Frontiers in Cellular and Infection Microbiology
one_line_summary: This review summarizes the interactions between gut microbiota and the gut-lung axis in ALI/ARDS, discussing mechanisms and therapeutic interventions including probiotics, prebiotics, FMT, and traditional Chinese herbal medicines.
key_pearls: ARDS has a **40%** overall in-hospital **mortality** rate, rising to **46.1%** for severe cases, with no definitive pharmacological treatment currently available.

Gut microbiota dysbiosis is a causal driver of ALI/ARDS, not merely a secondary consequence, validated by both antibiotic depletion (worsening injury) and FMT reconstitution (alleviating injury) studies.

SCFAs (acetate, propionate, butyrate) are the best-characterized functional metabolites in the gut-lung axis, regulating pulmonary immunity via GPCR activation, HDAC inhibition, and Treg differentiation.
FMT in ALI/ARDS models restores gut microbiota homeostasis, reduces pulmonary inflammation, and improves outcomes by modulating the TLR4/NF-κB pathway and Th17/Treg balance.
Probiotic strains including *Akkermansia muciniphila* and *Lactobacillus reuteri* alleviate ALI/ARDS by upregulating tight junction proteins (ZO-1, occludin) and promoting SCFA production.
Traditional Chinese herbal medicines (TCHMs) exert multi-level protection by correcting gut dysbiosis, enriching SCFA-producing bacteria, and suppressing the NF-κB/NLRP3 pathways.

Clinical translation of microbiota-targeted therapies faces challenges from ARDS heterogeneity, species differences in animal models, safety risks (probiotic bacteremia), and lack of validated predictive biomarkers.
population: Preclinical animal models and clinical observational studies of ALI/ARDS patients

sections.heading: Causal association between gut microbiota dysbiosis and ALI/ARDS
sections.content:
- The lungs harbor a stable, low-biomass microbial community, disproving the historical notion of lung sterility.
- Clinical ARDS patients exhibit gut dysbiosis and enrichment of gut-associated bacteria in the lung microbiota.
- Key lung microbiota changes in ARDS: increased **Enterobacteriaceae**, **Bacteroides**, **Staphylococcus**, **Streptococcus**, and **Lachnospiraceae**; decreased **Betaproteobacteria**.
- Key gut microbiota changes in ARDS: increased **Bacteroidetes**, **Proteobacteria**, **Enterobacteriaceae**, **Escherichia-Shigella**, and **Klebsiella pneumoniae**; decreased **Firmicutes**, **Bifidobacterium**, and SCFA-producing bacteria (e.g., *Roseburia inulinivorans*).
- In sepsis, a one-log increase in **Enterobacteriaceae** relative abundance within 7 days of ICU admission increases 180-day **mortality** risk by **92%**.
- Animal models recapitulate clinical dysbiosis: LPS, CLP, and burn injury increase gut-colonizing taxa in the lung (Bacteroidales, Enterobacteriaceae, Enterococcus) and decrease gut diversity.
- Reverse validation: Broad-spectrum antibiotic depletion of gut microbiota exacerbates *Streptococcus pneumoniae*-induced lung injury and **mortality**, impairing alveolar macrophage phagocytosis.
- Forward validation: FMT restores gut microbiota homeostasis (increasing Actinobacteria, Bacteroidetes, Firmicutes; decreasing Proteobacteria), reduces pulmonary inflammation, and improves outcomes via TLR4/NF-κB, Nrf2/HO-1, and JAK/STAT pathways.
- Causal dysbiosis occurs when microbial changes precede lung injury; secondary dysbiosis occurs when pulmonary inflammation drives gut changes. Non-pneumonic sepsis ARDS tends toward causal dysbiosis, while pneumonic ARDS involves more secondary dysbiosis.
- Limitations: Clinical studies are confounded by broad-spectrum antibiotic exposure, mechanical ventilation contamination, and potential reverse causality. Animal models have divergent microbiota from humans and cannot fully replicate clinical ARDS heterogeneity.
section_pearls: Gut and lung microbiota dysbiosis are highly correlated in ALI/ARDS, with enrichment of gut-associated bacteria in the lung.
FMT is the gold-standard method to validate causality between gut dysbiosis and ALI/ARDS.

sections.heading: Core mechanisms of immune communication along the gut-lung axis
sections.content:
- The gut-lung axis involves three hierarchical modules: upstream direct immune activation by microbial structural components, core remote immunomodulation by metabolites, and downstream immune cell circuits.
- Direct immune activation pathway: Intestinal barrier damage allows translocation of PAMPs (LPS, peptidoglycan) and DAMPs (HMGB1, eCIRP, ATP, cfDNA) via mesenteric lymphatics and portal vein to the lungs.
- Pulmonary PRRs (TLRs, NLRs) recognize translocated PAMPs; e.g., Bacteroides-derived LPS binds TLR4 on alveolar macrophages, activating NF-κB, MAPK, and NLRP3 inflammasome pathways.
- This triggers pro-inflammatory cytokine transcription (TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-6), neutrophil infiltration, and alveolar-capillary membrane damage.
- SCFAs (acetate, propionate, butyrate): Produced by gut bacterial fermentation of dietary fiber; account for >95% of intestinal SCFAs. Acetate via acetyl-CoA/Wood-Ljungdahl pathways; propionate via succinate/acrylate pathways; butyrate via butyrate kinase pathway.
- SCFA mechanisms: (1) Activate GPCRs (GPR41, GPR43, GPR109a) to inhibit pro-inflammatory cytokines; (2) Inhibit HDACs for epigenetic suppression of inflammatory gene transcription; (3) Enhance Treg differentiation by inhibiting JNK1 and p38 pathways.
- SCFAs regulate innate immune cells (suppress monocyte/DC maturation, balance ILC3/ILC2, regulate neutrophil apoptosis and NETosis) and adaptive immune cells (promote Treg generation, drive B cell plasma differentiation and SIgA secretion).
- SCFAs directly protect pulmonary endothelial and epithelial cells, inhibit DAMP release, and reduce vascular leakage.
- Bile acids (BAs): Synthesized via classical (CYP7A1) and alternative (CYP27A1) pathways; gut bacteria convert primary BAs (CA, CDCA) to secondary BAs (DCA, LCA). ARDS patients show elevated primary BAs and reduced secondary BAs.
- BAs bind TGR5 on pulmonary macrophages and DCs, suppressing LPS-induced NF-κB signaling; accumulated primary BAs can also act as DAMPs activating NLRP3 inflammasome.
- Other metabolites: Indole-related substances activate AhR for immune tolerance; kynurenine restricts excessive T cell activation; succinate activates SUCNR1 on AMs inducing M1 polarization; TMAO activates NLRP3 inflammasome and NF-κB in AMs.
- ILC3s: Gut DCs sense commensals and induce IL-22-secreting ILC3 migration to lungs; SCFAs enhance ILC3 function via PI3K/AKT/mTOR. IL-22 enhances epithelial barrier; IL-17A promotes bacterial phagocytosis.
- ILC2s: Account for 30% of lung ILCs; gut-to-lung trafficking depends on S1P signaling. Bidirectional role: IL-5 aggravates injury; IL-9 inhibits pyroptosis and alleviates ALI.
- Alveolar macrophages (AMs): 90-95% of lung immune cells; gut microbiota depletion impairs AM function, restorable by FMT. Sepsis-induced HIF-1α stabilization switches metabolism to glycolysis, driving M1 polarization. SCFAs (butyrate) reverse this via PPAR-γ, HIF-2α, PI3K/Akt1, promoting M2 polarization.
- Neutrophils: Gut bacterial translocation causes accumulation of apoptosis-resistant neutrophils in lungs. Gut dysbiosis expands pro-inflammatory Ly**6 G**+CD11bhi low-density neutrophils enhancing NETosis. Acetate inhibits neutrophil apoptosis; butyrate suppresses NETs formation.
- DCs: Sample gut microbial antigens and present to pulmonary T cells; gut metabolites (secondary BAs, tryptophan metabolites, SCFAs) inhibit DC maturation and induce tolerogenic phenotype.
- Adaptive immunity: FMT and SCFAs restore Th17/Treg balance via JAK/STAT pathway. Memory γδT17 cells migrate to lungs secreting IL-17A. MAIT cells activated by riboflavin metabolites enhance antibacterial immunity.
- Limitations: Species differences limit clinical translatability; studies overemphasize single pathways neglecting complex network interactions; relative contributions of PAMPs vs DAMPs pathways undefined across ARDS etiologies; individual microbiota heterogeneity limits precision intervention.
section_pearls: SCFAs are the core non-redundant metabolites mediating gut-lung communication, regulating both innate and adaptive pulmonary immunity.
AM metabolic reprogramming (OXPHOS to glycolysis) is a central mechanism driving M1 polarization in sepsis-induced ALI.

sections.heading: Microbiota-targeted therapeutic strategies for ALI/ARDS
sections.content:
- Core rationale: (1) Restore gut eubiosis; (2) Repair intestinal barrier to reduce bacterial translocation; (3) Enhance beneficial metabolites (SCFAs, secondary BAs); (4) Modulate systemic and pulmonary immune responses.
- Probiotics: Validated strains include *Akkermansia muciniphila*, *Lactobacillus reuteri*, *Weissella cibaria*, *Bacteroides acidifaciens*, and SCFA-producing mixtures.
- Probiotic mechanisms: Downregulate Bacteroidetes, upregulate Firmicutes; promote SCFA (butyrate) production; inhibit NF-κB pathways; modulate macrophage and neutrophil phenotypes; upregulate intestinal barrier proteins (ZO-1, occludin, MUC2).
- Clinical translation: RCT (NCT030745552) found *B. subtilis* and *E. faecalis* effective for VAP prevention; RCT (NCT02462590) found no **significant** effect of *Lactobacillus rhamnosus* GG on VAP. Negative outcomes attributed to low colonization efficiency, late intervention timing, and lack of patient stratification.
- Probiotic safety risks: Cases of *Lactobacillus* bacteremia and sepsis in immunosuppressed or intestinal barrier-impaired patients; retrospective cohort study found increased **mortality** with probiotic use in ICU patients with central venous catheters.
- Prebiotics: Inulin, FOS, resistant starch stimulate SCFA-producing bacteria. FOS enhances *Bifidobacterium* abundance; long-chain inulin and resistant starch increase circulating SCFAs. Phase II RCT in ICU sepsis patients found inulin safe but without substantial clinical benefit.
- FMT: Restores gut microbiota-SCFAs axis, enhances AM bactericidal ability, inhibits TLR4/NF-κB, restores Th17/Treg balance. Clinical case reports show efficacy for refractory multidrug-resistant infections.
- FMT challenges: Lack of standardized donor selection, preparation protocols, and administration methods; iatrogenic infection risk in immunosuppressed patients; individual heterogeneity in colonization efficacy; no large-scale RCTs in ALI/ARDS.
- TCHMs: Multi-level protection via three pathways: (1) Correct gut dysbiosis and preserve intestinal barrier integrity; (2) Enrich beneficial commensals and mediate pulmonary immunomodulation via SCFAs and BAs; (3) Regulate key immune cell circuits and inflammatory signaling pathways.
- TCHMs barrier protection: Sinomenine, JHD, QYD, YZC, XFBD, BYTQ, HCPM upregulate ZO-1, occludin, Muc-1, Claudin-1; reduce serum LPS, D-LA, DAO; inhibit pathogenic bacteria (Citrobacter, Enterococcus, Escherichia-Shigella).
- TCHMs beneficial bacteria enrichment: RES, rhubarb, baicalin, AS-IV, YHPG upregulate *Lactobacillus*; KQ, QYD, YZC, JFG, HupA, HCPM enrich *Akkermansia muciniphila*; multiple TCHMs increase SCFA-producing bacteria (Alistipes, Bacteroides, Bifidobacterium, Blautia, Roseburia).
- TCHMs metabolite modulation: RES, GQD, WRS, total ginsenosides, BAI, RRFJ, HCPM, YHPG increase acetate, propionate, butyrate, valeric acid. CMME and SLEL regulate BA profiles (taurocholic acid, cholic acid, deoxycholic acid).
- TCHMs immune regulation: miR-7972, FZJDF, EEP/EEG, FZQS, CMME, GCP-2 drive M1 to M2 macrophage switch; JHD reduces neutrophil and γδT cell infiltration; RES regulates CTL, NKT, Treg cells; rhubarb modulates Th17/Treg balance.
- TCHMs signaling pathways: XBCQ, baicalin, HEL, YZC, BBP, EEP/EEG, NDC, FZQS, HupA, SMI, LXHX, GCP-2 suppress NF-κB and NLRP3 inflammasome. Sinomenine activates intestinal AhR/Nrf2; AS-IV and BYTQ modulate PI3K/AKT/mTOR; WRS and BAI upregulate FFAR2/FFAR3; rhubarb activates HDAC6.
section_pearls: Probiotic efficacy in critically ill patients is highly strain-specific and dependent on baseline gut microbiota composition and intervention timing.
TCHMs exert microbiota-targeted effects comparable to probiotics and FMT, with multi-component advantages addressing individual heterogeneity.

sections.heading: Translational challenges and future perspectives
sections.content:
- Core translational challenges: (1) ARDS heterogeneity—different etiologies (sepsis, trauma, viral) have distinct microbiota and immune profiles, making one-size-fits-all strategies ineffective. (2) Gap between SPF rodent models and clinical reality—differences in microbiota composition, lack of comorbidities, and standardized modeling fail to replicate **critically** ill patient complexity. (3) Safety risks—probiotic bacteremia and FMT iatrogenic infection in patients with impaired intestinal barrier and immunosuppression; TCHMs' multi-component nature complicates distinguishing direct pharmacological effects from microbiota-mediated effects. (4) Lack of validated biomarkers to predict efficacy and stratify patients for microbiota-targeted interventions.
- Future directions: (1) Construct clear conceptual framework—elucidate how gut-derived signals influence pulmonary innate immunity; use single-cell multi-omics to analyze cell-type-specific regulatory mechanisms. (2) Develop multi-omics and AI-based precision patient stratification—integrate metagenomics, metabolomics, transcriptomics, and immunophenotyping; use machine learning to identify microbiota-associated ARDS phenotypes and predict intervention efficacy. (3) Optimize intervention strategies—develop colon-targeted delivery systems for SCFAs and prebiotics; construct engineered probiotics; develop gut-targeted TCHMs delivery systems (e.g., pH-sensitive microcapsules for baicalin); conduct large-scale multicenter RCTs with strict biomarker-based patient stratification. (4) Explore synergistic TCHMs-combination strategies—combine TCHMs with probiotics/prebiotics (e.g., baicalin with inulin) to enhance microbiota modulation and address individual heterogeneity.
section_pearls: Multi-omics integration with AI is essential for identifying microbiota-associated ARDS phenotypes and enabling precision microbiota-targeted therapies.
Synergistic combinations of TCHMs with probiotics/prebiotics may overcome the limitations of single-intervention strategies.

strengths_limitations:
- Strengths: Comprehensive systematic review covering causal association, mechanistic immune circuits, and therapeutic strategies; integrates clinical and preclinical evidence across probiotics, prebiotics, FMT, and TCHMs; provides **critical** evaluation of study limitations and translational challenges.
- Limitations: Majority of mechanistic evidence derived from rodent models with **significant** species differences from humans; clinical observational studies confounded by antibiotic exposure and mechanical ventilation; lack of large-scale RCTs for most microbiota-targeted interventions in ALI/ARDS; relative contributions of PAMPs vs DAMPs pathways undefined across different ARDS etiologies.
